Subarachnoid hemorrhage Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Aqueductal stenosis leads to dilation of the lateral and third ventricles with a small fourth ventricle, not isolated lateral ventricular enlargement. It is also a more chronic process. Choice B: Cerebellar tonsillar herniation results from severe intracranial pressure and causes brainstem compression with coma or death, but does not explain isolated ventricular enlargement patterns. Choice C: Choroid plexus papilloma increases CSF production, causing communicating hydrocephalus, typically with enlargement of all ventricles. It does not obstruct a specific foramen. Choice E: Ependymoma of the fourth ventricle causes obstruction at the level of the fourth ventricle or its outlets, resulting in dilation of the third and lateral ventricles. The fourth ventricle would be enlarged or irregular, unlike in this case. Choice F: Meningiomas are slow-growing and often asymptomatic until large. Compression of the aqueduct would cause triventricular hydrocephalus (lateral + third ventricles enlarged), not isolated lateral ventricular dilation. Choice G: Pinealomas can compress the pretectal region or the cerebral aqueduct, causing Parinaud syndrome or aqueductal obstruction. Again, they would lead to enlargement of lateral and third ventricles, not just the lateral ones. Choice H: Subarachnoid hemorrhage impairs CSF reabsorption and causes communicating hydrocephalus with global ventricular enlargement. It does not produce the obstructive pattern seen here. Bottom line: A colloid cyst of the foramen of Monro causes acute obstructive hydrocephalus with selective enlargement of the lateral ventricles, often in young adults presenting with positional headache, vomiting, and sudden collapse. Choice B: The median nerve (C5–T1) travels with the brachial artery in the arm, passes between the pronator teres heads, and enters the hand via the carpal tunnel. It supplies motor innervation to most forearm flexors and the thenar muscles via the recurrent branch (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis), and provides sensory input to the lateral palm and palmar aspects of the lateral 3½ digits (plus dorsal distal tips). Injury leads to thenar weakness, loss of thumb opposition, and sensory deficits; carpal tunnel compression causes nocturnal paresthesias and thenar atrophy. Because it contains critical motor fibers for hand function, a biopsy here risks disabling motor impairment and is inappropriate when aiming to avoid motor dysfunction.
A. Common fibular nerve B. Median nerve C. Radial nerve D. Sural nerve E. Ulnar nerve Cutaneous nerves of the hand. Choice C: The radial nerve (C5–T1) courses in the spiral groove with the profunda brachii artery, innervates the triceps, then splits into deep (posterior interosseous) and superficial branches near the lateral epicondyle. It is the primary motor supply to wrist and finger extensors, with sensory coverage to the posterior arm/forearm and dorsal lateral hand. Injury produces the HY finding of wrist drop and loss of finger extension, often with dorsal hand numbness; compression can occur with prolonged pressure (“Saturday night palsy”). Because biopsy would endanger essential motor function for wrist and finger extension, this nerve is not selected when preserving motor strength is a priority. Choice E: The ulnar nerve (C8–T1) passes posterior to the medial epicondyle (funny bone), enters the forearm between the FCU heads, and reaches the hand via Guyon’s canal. It supplies motor fibers to the intrinsic hand muscles (interossei, medial lumbricals) and forearm flexors (FCU and medial FDP) and provides sensory innervation to the medial 1½ digits and corresponding hand regions, palmar and dorsal. Lesions cause grip weakness, loss of finger abduction/adduction, and ulnar claw posture with sensory loss along the medial hand. Because of its significant motor role in intrinsic hand function, ulnar nerve biopsy risks permanent motor deficits and is not appropriate when motor preservation is required. Bottom line: The sural nerve is a purely sensory nerve located along the posterior-lateral leg and lateral foot. Its lack of motor fibers allows biopsy without causing muscle weakness, making it the preferred site when histopathologic confirmation of a neuropathy is required. This approach preserves motor function while still allowing for definitive diagnosis of conditions such as vasculitis, amyloidosis, leprosy, or hereditary neuropathies. Visual cortex Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The ciliary ganglion relays parasympathetic fibers from the oculomotor nerve to the sphincter pupillae, enabling pupillary constriction. A lesion here would result in a dilated, poorly reactive pupil on the same side, due to disruption of the efferent pathway. However, this patient has normal constriction when light is directed into the left eye, indicating that the left ciliary ganglion is functioning properly. The paradoxical dilation when light is moved to the right eye suggests a right-sided afferent defect, not an efferent issue on the left. Choice B: The oculomotor nerve carries motor fibers for extraocular muscles and parasympathetic fibers to the pupil. Damage would cause ptosis, "down and out" eye deviation, and a fixed, dilated pupil on the affected side. None of these findings are present on physical exam. Additionally, the left pupil constricts normally to light, confirming that the left oculomotor nerve is intact. Choice C: The optic tract carries post-chiasmal visual input from both eyes and lesions here usually produce contralateral homonymous hemianopia. Although severe optic tract lesions can occasionally cause an RAPD, it would be on the side opposite the lesion. In this case, light shown into the left eye produces a normal response, and the defect is clearly right-sided, making a left optic tract lesion unlikely. Choice D: The Edinger-Westphal nucleus sends parasympathetic output through the oculomotor nerve to mediate pupillary constriction. A lesion here would impair constriction of the right pupil, but the left pupil would still constrict when either eye is stimulated. In this case, both pupils fail to constrict when light is shown into the right eye, which is consistent with impaired afferent input, not efferent output. The right pupil also constricts when light is shown in the left eye, which further rules out an efferent lesion. Choice F: The visual cortex processes visual perception but is not involved in the pupillary light reflex, which is a subcortical reflex mediated through the midbrain. Damage here would result in loss of conscious visual awareness in specific visual fields, but the pupillary response to light would remain intact. Since the reflex is mediated by pretectal and midbrain nuclei before reaching the cortex, lesions in the visual cortex cannot explain the absent constriction with right-eye stimulation. Pathophysiologically, strabismus arises from either an imbalance in extraocular muscle strength, abnormal cranial nerve function (commonly CN III, IV, or VI), or congenital defects in ocular muscle anatomy. The clinical hallmark is ocular misalignment, which can present as esotropia, exotropia, hypertropia, or hypotropia. Children may develop
A. Amblyopia compensatory head postures or close one eye to avoid diplopia. The risk of permanent visual impairment underscores the importance of early diagnosis and intervention. B. Astigmatism Early detection via the cover-uncover test and prompt management with patching, corrective lenses, or surgical intervention is critical to prevent amblyopia and preserve binocular vision. C. Hyperopia Wrong answer explanations: D. Myopia Choice A: Amblyopia refers to decreased visual acuity in one or both eyes due to abnormal visual development, often secondary to strabismus, refractive errors, or visual deprivation. The condition is a result of cortical suppression of the image from the less-used eye, leading to poor vision that cannot be corrected by glasses alone. Amblyopia is not E. Strabismus the primary cause of ocular misalignment or diplopia; rather, it is a potential complication of untreated strabismus. Clinically, amblyopia presents as unilateral or bilateral decreased vision without a structural abnormality of the eye, and children typically do not have diplopia since the brain suppresses the weaker image. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying cause (e.g., patching the dominant eye) to force use of the amblyopic eye and stimulate visual development. Choice B: Astigmatism is caused by an irregular curvature of the cornea or lens, resulting in distorted or blurred vision at all distances. The abnormal shape leads to light focusing at multiple points on the retina, compromising image clarity. This condition does not cause eye misalignment, diplopia, or abnormal cover-uncover test findings. Astigmatism commonly presents with visual blurring, headaches, and eyestrain, but does not produce diplopia or strabismus. It is corrected with cylindrical lenses that compensate for the irregular refractive power of the cornea or lens. Choice C: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short anteroposteriorly or the cornea has insufficient curvature, causing light to focus behind the retina. Patients with hyperopia see distant objects more clearly than near objects, often leading to difficulty with close work. Children may compensate with increased accommodation, but this can cause eyestrain or headaches. Hyperopia does not cause ocular misalignment or diplopia, but uncorrected severe hyperopia can predispose to accommodative esotropia, a specific form of strabismus, which may overlap with this presentation but is not the general mechanism described here. Clinical presentation is typically blurry near vision, not double vision or shifting ocular fixation. Choice D: Myopia (nearsightedness) is due to an elongated eyeball or excessive corneal curvature, resulting in light focusing in front of the retina. This causes distant objects to appear blurry while near vision remains clear. Myopia does not cause strabismus or issues with binocular alignment, and patients do not exhibit abnormal findings on cover- uncover testing. Severe myopia increases the risk of retinal detachment due to elongation of the globe, but does not account for the described diplopia or misalignment. Clinical features center around blurry distance vision and frequent squinting, not double vision or depth perception loss. Bottom line: Strabismus is a disorder of ocular alignment that results in binocular vision loss, diplopia, and impaired depth perception, and can cause amblyopia if untreated. Differentiating strabismus from refractive errors like astigmatism, hyperopia, and myopia is critical, as only strabismus presents with eye misalignment and abnormal cover-uncover test. Clinically, amblyopia presents as unilateral or bilateral decreased vision without a structural abnormality of the eye, and children typically do not have diplopia since the brain suppresses the weaker image. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying cause (e.g., patching the dominant eye) to force use of the amblyopic eye and stimulate visual development. Choice B: Astigmatism is caused by an irregular curvature of the cornea or lens, resulting in distorted or blurred vision at all distances. The abnormal shape leads to light focusing at multiple points on the retina, compromising image clarity. This condition does not cause eye misalignment, diplopia, or abnormal cover-uncover test findings. Astigmatism commonly presents with visual blurring, headaches, and eyestrain, but does not produce diplopia or strabismus. It is corrected with cylindrical lenses that compensate for the irregular refractive power of the cornea or lens. Choice C: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short anteroposteriorly or the cornea has insufficient curvature, causing light to focus behind the retina. Patients with hyperopia see distant objects more clearly than near objects, often leading to difficulty with close work. Children may compensate with increased accommodation, but this can cause eyestrain or headaches. Hyperopia does not cause ocular misalignment or diplopia, but uncorrected severe hyperopia can predispose to accommodative esotropia, a specific form of strabismus, which may overlap with this presentation but is not the general mechanism described here. Clinical presentation is typically blurry near vision, not double vision or shifting ocular fixation. Choice D: Myopia (nearsightedness) is due to an elongated eyeball or excessive corneal curvature, resulting in light focusing in front of the retina. This causes distant objects to appear blurry while near vision remains clear. Myopia does not cause strabismus or issues with binocular alignment, and patients do not exhibit abnormal findings on cover- uncover testing. Severe myopia increases the risk of retinal detachment due to elongation of the globe, but does not account for the described diplopia or misalignment. Clinical features center around blurry distance vision and frequent squinting, not double vision or depth perception loss. Bottom line: Strabismus is a disorder of ocular alignment that results in binocular vision loss, diplopia, and impaired depth perception, and can cause amblyopia if untreated. Differentiating strabismus from refractive errors like astigmatism, hyperopia, and myopia is critical, as only strabismus presents with eye misalignment and abnormal cover-uncover test. Choice C: Medial thigh pain follows the obturator nerve (L2–L4), often implicated in obturator hernias, pelvic sidewall pathology, or hip osteoarthritis causing adductor spasm. This distribution is not the canonical referral pattern for ureteral colic, which tracks sympathetic afferents from T10–L2 and shifts flank → lower quadrant → groin as the stone descends. Because the obturator territory is not the typical target for distal ureteral pain, medial thigh radiation is not the best answer here. Choice D: The perineum is innervated predominantly by the pudendal nerve (S2–S4) and is a hallmark referral zone for anorectal disease, pelvic floor disorders, perineal abscess, and urogenital conditions centered in the distal urethra or external genitalia. Ureteral visceral afferents do not travel with sacral parasympathetics; instead they accompany sympathetic fibers to T10–L2, explaining why perineal radiation is not expected in ureteric stone pain. Given this distinct neuroanatomy, perineal localization is inconsistent with the scenario of migrating ureteral colic. Choice E: The shoulder is a common site of referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation via the phrenic nerve (C3–C5), seen in conditions such as subphrenic abscess, splenic rupture, or peritonitis involving the diaphragm. This dermatomal distribution is entirely unrelated to ureteral innervation, which travels via sympathetic fibers from T10–L2. Because the neuroanatomic pathways are completely different, shoulder radiation would not occur in ureteral colic, making this choice entirely incorrect for this patient’s presentation. Bottom line: Ureteral colic from nephrolithiasis produces severe, colicky flank pain that changes location as the stone migrates, reflecting the T10–L2 visceral afferent pathway. When the stone reaches the distal ureter, pain is referred to the groin via shared innervation with the genitofemoral nerve (L1–L2). Anatomy Neurology (Innervation of Other organ systems) None

A. A B. B C. C D. D Choice A = hippocampus; B = crus cerebri; C = ventral medial nucleus of thalamus; D = substantia nigra; E = subthalamic nucleus; F = ventral lateral nucleus of thalamus. E. E F. F Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The hippocampus is involved in learning and memory consolidation and is classically affected early in Alzheimer disease, leading to progressive memory impairment and disorientation. Lesions of the hippocampus do not cause hemiballismus or other hyperkinetic movement disorders. Infarcts here would more likely present with profound anterograde amnesia. The hippocampus receives its primary vascular supply from branches of the posterior cerebral artery, but damage here would not explain this patient’s hyperkinetic presentation. Choice B: The crus cerebri contains descending corticospinal and corticobulbar fibers transmitting voluntary motor commands from the cortex to the brainstem and spinal cord. Lesions here typically produce contralateral hemiparesis and may also affect cranial nerve fibers depending on rostrocaudal location, but they do not produce involuntary hyperkinetic movements. The classic vascular lesion here is a stroke of penetrating branches of the posterior cerebral artery. The hallmark would be weakness, not ballistic movements. Choice C: The ventral medial nucleus of the thalamus relays sensory information from the trigeminal and spinothalamic tracts to the primary somatosensory cortex. Damage to this thalamic region would result in contralateral hemisensory loss and possibly thalamic pain syndrome but not involuntary movement disorders. The thalamus is often affected in hypertensive lacunar strokes but would present with pure sensory stroke features rather than hemiballismus. Lesions here are typically localized by dense hemisensory deficits. Choice D: The substantia nigra is a basal ganglia structure located in the midbrain that contains dopaminergic neurons projecting to the striatum and is classically degenerated in Parkinson disease, leading to bradykinesia, rigidity, and resting tremor. Damage to this area causes hypokinetic rather than hyperkinetic movement disorders. It is not typically implicated in hemiballismus. The substantia nigra’s role in dopaminergic modulation distinguishes it from the indirect pathway disruption seen in this case. Choice F: The ventral lateral nucleus of the thalamus serves as a relay station for motor signals from the cerebellum and basal ganglia to the motor cortex, and lesions here can result in deficits of coordination and fine motor control, but would not typically cause large-amplitude ballistic movements. Damage here could mimic a cerebellar lesion but would present with more subtle deficits like dysmetria and intention tremor. The ventral lateral nucleus is an output relay rather than a direct modulator of basal ganglia indirect pathway signaling. Bottom line: Hemiballismus is characterized by violent, flinging, proximal limb movements contralateral to a lesion of the subthalamic nucleus, most commonly caused by hypertensive lacunar infarction. Recognition of this rare but localizing movement disorder emphasizes the importance of the basal ganglia indirect pathway in movement suppression. Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) Choice A: Toxoplasmosis is the most common cause of ring-enhancing brain lesions in AIDS but is less likely if the patient is on Pneumocystis prophylaxis with TMP-SMX, since TMP-SMX is also the prophylaxis for Toxo. Toxoplasma gondii causes encephalitis via reactivation of latent cysts when CD4 counts fall below 100. It usually presents with multiple ring-enhancing lesions and mass effect. Empiric treatment with pyrimethamine and sulfadiazine is initiated in typical cases. Choice B: Cryptococcal infection primarily causes subacute meningitis in AIDS patients with CD4 <100, characterized by fever, headache, and elevated intracranial pressure. It does not typically present with ring-enhancing lesions on imaging but rather with diffuse meningeal enhancement. CSF analysis is the key diagnostic step showing cryptococcal antigen or encapsulated yeasts on India ink stain. Neurologic deficits are due to meningeal involvement and hydrocephalus, not focal brain lesions. Choice C: HSV-1 encephalitis is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis in adults. It preferentially involves the temporal lobes and presents with fever, headache, seizures, and altered mental status. Imaging shows hyperintense lesions in the temporal lobes with associated edema and hemorrhagic necrosis, not solitary ring-enhancing lesions. It is treated with intravenous acyclovir and should be suspected when behavioral changes and seizures predominate. Choice E: PML is caused by reactivation of JC virus and presents in advanced immunosuppression with progressive cognitive and motor decline. Imaging shows non-enhancing white matter lesions without mass effect, primarily affecting parietal and occipital lobes. There is no ring-enhancing lesion on CT or MRI, which differentiates it from primary CNS lymphoma and toxoplasmosis. CSF PCR can confirm JC virus infection. Bottom line: TMP-SMX is the prophylaxis for both Pneumocystis and Toxo. So if a ring-enhancing lesion is seen in a patient with CD4 count under 100 who is already on TMP-SMX, the USMLE wants primary CNS lymphoma, not Toxo. It is not mandatory Toxo is multiple lesions. Occasionally it can be a single lesion. What's more important is you know the prophylaxis for Pnemocystis is two birds with one stone for Toxo also. Germinomas are more homogenous and solid, often showing positive PLAP on biopsy. Serum markers like β-hCG or AFP may aid diagnosis. Choice E: Medulloblastomas are aggressive midline posterior fossa tumors, most commonly arising in the cerebellar vermis of children. They present with truncal ataxia, morning headache, nausea, and signs of hydrocephalus due to fourth ventricle compression. MRI shows a midline hyperdense mass in the posterior fossa, and histology reveals small, round blue cells with Homer Wright rosettes. This patient has no cerebellar or vestibular findings, and the mass location is suprasellar—not posterior fossa—making this diagnosis unlikely. Front:
Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient’s condition?
A. Antibody-mediated destruction of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction B. Antibody-mediated destruction of pre-synaptic voltage-gated calcium channels C. Autoimmune destruction of Schwann cells and peripheral myelin D. T-cell-mediated attack on oligodendrocytes E. Viral infection leading to destruction of anterior horn motor neurons Multifocal hyperintense plaques, indicating MS. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Antibody-mediated destruction of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction describes the pathophysiology of myasthenia gravis, an autoimmune disorder characterized by fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness that worsens with exertion and improves with rest. Patients often present with ptosis, diplopia, and difficulty chewing or swallowing. The mechanism involves autoantibodies blocking postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors, impairing neuromuscular transmission. Myasthenia gravis does not cause sensory disturbances or demyelinating CNS lesions. MRI findings would be normal in myasthenia gravis. Choice B: Antibody-mediated destruction of postsynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels describes Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), a paraneoplastic autoimmune condition commonly associated with small cell lung cancer. In LEMS, impaired calcium influx reduces acetylcholine release at neuromuscular junctions, leading to proximal muscle weakness that improves with repeated use (facilitation). Autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth are common. LEMS is a presynaptic disorder distinct from MS and does not cause relapsing CNS demyelination or characteristic white matter lesions on MRI. Choice C: Autoimmune destruction of Schwann cells and peripheral myelin describes Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS), an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy often triggered by preceding infection. GBS leads to rapidly progressive ascending weakness, areflexia, and sometimes respiratory failure. It is a peripheral nervous system disorder that spares the central nervous system and is not associated with oligoclonal bands in CSF or disseminated white matter lesions. MRI in GBS would not show CNS plaques as seen in MS. Choice E: Viral infection of anterior horn cells with resulting neuronal necrosis describes poliomyelitis, caused by poliovirus infection leading to destruction of lower motor neurons. Poliomyelitis presents with asymmetric flaccid paralysis, muscle atrophy, and decreased reflexes without sensory loss. It primarily affects motor neurons in the anterior horn of the spinal cord and does not involve demyelination of white matter or produce characteristic MS plaques on MRI. There is no relapsing-remitting course in poliomyelitis. Bottom line: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune demyelinating disease caused by T-cell–mediated attack on oligodendrocytes, resulting in relapsing-remitting neurologic deficits and multifocal CNS plaques on MRI. Management includes acute corticosteroids for flares and disease-modifying therapy to reduce relapse risk and progression. Physiology Neurology (Autoimmune conditions) None Subject System Topic

D. T-cell-mediated attack on oligodendrocytes

A. Anosmia on the affected side B. Hyperacusis on the affected side C. Loss of gag reflex on the affected side D. Loss of taste sensation on the posterior third of the tongue E. Ptosis with impaired pupillary constriction on the affected side UMNL VS LMNL of facial nerve. Bell’s palsy is idiopathic in most cases but is thought to be related to viral reactivation (especially herpes simplex virus type 1) causing inflammation and edema of the facial nerve in the facial canal. Other causes of LMN facial palsy include Lyme disease, Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster virus), sarcoidosis, HIV, otitis media, and neoplasms compressing the nerve. Bilateral LMN palsy is rare but can be seen in Guillain-Barré syndrome and Lyme disease. Clinical features include inability to close the eye (risking exposure keratitis), drooping of the mouth, loss of nasolabial fold, drooling, decreased tearing, hyperacusis, and loss of taste sensation from the anterior tongue. Management of Bell’s palsy includes early administration of high-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammation and improve nerve recovery; antivirals (eg, acyclovir) may be added if viral reactivation is suspected. Eye protection with lubricating drops and taping shut during sleep is crucial to prevent corneal damage. Most patients recover within weeks to months, but incomplete recovery or synkinesis (involuntary movement during voluntary contraction) can occur. Identifying secondary causes (eg, through serologic testing for Lyme disease in endemic areas) is important to guide targeted therapy. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Anosmia localizes to cranial nerve I or the olfactory bulbs/tracts at the cribriform plate and frontal base; common etiologies include head trauma with shearing of olfactory fila, viral URIs, sinonasal disease, and frontal lobe lesions. The facial nerve (VII) mediates facial expression, stapedius function, parasympathetics to lacrimal/submandibular/sublingual glands, and taste to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani—none of which generate true loss of smell. In LMN facial palsy, patients may report altered taste or dry eye/mouth, but olfaction is intact. Anosmia would not be expected in isolated Bell palsy, making this choice inconsistent with the lesion described. Choice C: The gag reflex depends on glossopharyngeal nerve (IX) afferents from the posterior oropharynx and vagus nerve (X) efferents to the pharyngeal constrictors; lesions of IX/X (eg, jugular foramen syndrome, brainstem stroke) abolish the reflex and cause dysphagia, dysphonia, and uvular deviation. The facial nerve (VII) does not contribute to the gag reflex circuitry. In Bell palsy, bulbar signs are absent; speech and swallowing are preserved apart from oral incompetence from lip weakness. Loss of gag reflex would indicate a different cranial neuropathy or lateral medullary pathology rather than an isolated facial nerve palsy. Choice D: Taste from the posterior one-third of the tongue is carried by cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) via the lingual branch and the inferior (petrosal) ganglion to the nucleus solitarius; CN IX also supplies the carotid sinus/body and motor to stylopharyngeus. Bell palsy affects taste from the anterior two-thirds through the chorda tympani (VII) and may reduce salivation from submandibular/sublingual glands, but it spares CN IX territory. Loss of posterior third taste points to glossopharyngeal dysfunction (eg, skull base lesion, post-tonsillectomy injury), not an LMN facial neuropathy. Choice E: Ptosis with impaired pupillary constriction reflects oculomotor (CN III) dysfunction involving levator palpebrae superioris and parasympathetic fibers from the Edinger–Westphal nucleus; additional signs include a “down-and-out” eye and diplopia, while sympathetic lesions (Horner) cause mild ptosis with miosis and anhidrosis. In Bell palsy, the eyelid cannot close due to orbicularis oculi weakness (VII), producing lagophthalmos and exposure keratopathy—not true ptosis. Pupillary light responses remain normal because parasympathetic pathways travel with CN III, not CN VII. These findings separate facial nerve palsy from third-nerve or sympathetic lesions. Bottom line: Bell palsy is an LMN lesion of the ipsilateral CN VII causing complete unilateral facial weakness, loss of anterior tongue taste, impaired tear and saliva production, and sometimes hyperacusis from stapedius paralysis. Hyperacusis results from loss of ossicle-dampening by the stapedius. UMN lesions spare the forehead due to dual innervation, but LMN lesions affect the whole side. Treatment is corticosteroids, eye protection, and addressing underlying causes. Anatomy Neurology (Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles) None
Back:
D. Loss of taste sensation on the posterior third of the tongue

Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, leading to fatigable muscle weakness. It typically presents with ptosis, diplopia, and bulbar symptoms, and progresses in a descending pattern. Reflexes and sensation are typically preserved, and there is no autonomic dysfunction. MG affects the neuromuscular junction, a key distinction from GBS, which affects the peripheral nervous system. Treatments include acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, corticosteroids, and immunomodulators. Choice E: Transverse myelitis is a focal inflammation of the spinal cord, leading to motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction below the lesion level. While it can present acutely with limb weakness, it is typically localized, and findings are often asymmetric. MRI shows a focal spinal cord lesion, and CSF usually shows pleocytosis, not the albuminocytologic dissociation characteristic of GBS. Transverse myelitis may mimic GBS but lacks the symmetric ascending weakness and peripheral nerve findings.

Choice D: Lambert-Eaton syndrome (LES) is a disorder of presynaptic neuromuscular transmission most often associated with paraneoplastic syndromes, particularly small cell lung carcinoma. It is caused by autoantibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels, impairing acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. Patients typically present with symmetric proximal muscle weakness that improves with repeated activity, in contrast to the fatigable weakness of myasthenia gravis. Hyporeflexia is common, and autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth, constipation, and erectile dysfunction frequently occur. Bulbar and ocular involvement is usually mild, and UMN signs like hyperreflexia and Babinski are not part of the disease profile. This patient’s brisk reflexes, lack of autonomic features, and combined UMN/LMN findings strongly argue against LES. Additionally, fasciculations and asymmetric atrophy are not characteristic of LES, further excluding it. Choice E: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system characterized by recurrent neurologic deficits separated in time and space. Common presentations include optic neuritis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, sensory disturbances, limb weakness, and bladder/bowel dysfunction. Reflexes are often brisk due to corticospinal tract involvement, but LMN signs such as fasciculations and muscle atrophy are not seen because MS does not cause degeneration of anterior horn cells. Sensory deficits are a hallmark of MS, including impaired vibration sense, proprioception, or pain/temperature perception, which are entirely absent in this patient. The steady, relentless progression without distinct relapses or remissions is more typical of ALS than MS. Finally, MRI findings in MS reveal demyelinating plaques in the brain or spinal cord, whereas ALS imaging is often normal or shows only nonspecific changes. The absence of sensory involvement and presence of LMN signs make MS very unlikely in this case.
A. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis B. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy Bottom line: ALS presents with a combination of upper and lower motor neuron signs without sensory deficits. A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of intermittent urine leakage

Front:
Choices A, B and C: Detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence all cause impaired bladder emptying, but their mechanisms and presentations differ from this patient’s case. Detrusor underactivity occurs when the bladder muscle cannot generate enough contractile force during micturition, often due to diabetic autonomic neuropathy, spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, or chronic overdistension. Outlet obstruction, in contrast, results from narrowing of the bladder neck or urethra—seen in conditions such as urethral stricture, pelvic tumor, bladder neck contracture, or benign prostatic hyperplasia in men—which raises detrusor pressures during voiding and causes hesitancy, weak stream, and postvoid dribbling. Both detrusor underactivity and outlet obstruction can lead to overflow incontinence, in which bladder capacity is exceeded and urine dribbles constantly; this is accompanied by a distended bladder, high postvoid residual volumes, and often recurrent urinary tract infections. In this patient, postvoid residual volume is normal, bladder emptying is not impaired, and symptoms are triggered by urgency rather than continuous leakage. There is no history of hesitancy, weak stream, straining to void, or incomplete emptying, and the course is not suggestive of chronic bladder dysfunction. These features make detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence unlikely explanations for her presentation.
A. Detrusor underactivity Choice E: UTIs cause inflammation of the bladder mucosa (cystitis) or renal collecting system (pyelonephritis), most often due to ascending Escherichia coli. Classic symptoms include dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort, and occasionally hematuria. Pyelonephritis presents with fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness. B. Outlet obstruction In older adults, UTIs may cause transient incontinence due to bladder irritation. However, this patient has no dysuria, fever, hematuria, suprapubic tenderness, or systemic symptoms, and her chronic symptom course is inconsistent with acute infection. C. Overflow incontinence Bottom line: D. Uninhibited bladder contractions Urge incontinence is due to involuntary detrusor muscle contractions from loss of inhibitory control, leading to sudden urgency and leakage—often with sensory triggers. Diagnosis is clinical, and treatment focuses on bladder training, trigger avoidance, and drugs that reduce detrusor overactivity. E. Choice F: This coordinates horizontal gaze by stimulating the ipsilateral abducens nucleus. A lesion here would prevent conjugate gaze toward the side of the lesion, not isolated internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Choice G: Compression can cause Parinaud syndrome (upward gaze palsy, light-near dissociation), not INO. It has no role in horizontal gaze. Choice H: Involved in reflex gaze shifts and vertical eye movements, but not in coordination of horizontal gaze between CN VI and CN III. Bottom line: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia is due to a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus, most often caused by multiple sclerosis in young patients, resulting in impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the abducting eye. Subject System Topic A 12-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off the monkey bars at school. He landed on his outstretched right hand and immediately felt pain at his elbow. He has no significant past medical history. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the medial aspect of the right elbow. He keeps the arm flexed and resists attempts at extension due to pain. There is decreased
A. Central retinal artery occlusion B. Guillain-Barré syndrome C. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia D. Myasthenia gravis E. Optic neuritis Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents as sudden, painless, complete monocular vision loss, often associated with a cherry-red spot on fundus exam and a history of vascular risk factors (e.g., atrial fibrillation, carotid atherosclerosis). It does not cause eye pain, afferent pupillary defect, or signs of demyelination elsewhere in the CNS, such as clonus or urinary incontinence. CRAO is an ophthalmologic emergency but is not associated with demyelinating plaques or young women. Choice B: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy affecting the peripheral nervous system, with ascending weakness, areflexia, and possible autonomic instability. GBS does not involve central demyelination, optic neuritis, or upper motor neuron findings. It typically follows a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni) and is managed with IVIG or plasmapheresis. GBS spares the optic nerve, which is part of the CNS. Choice C: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results from demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), also seen in MS. INO is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the contralateral eye during attempted lateral gaze. Unlike optic neuritis, INO does not present with acute vision loss or pain; instead, patients complain of diplopia. Clinical examination shows preserved pupillary light reflex, distinguishing it from optic neuritis. INO is highly associated with MS but is not the best match for the patient's acute monocular visual loss and afferent pupillary defect. Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction, causing fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigability, especially of ocular and bulbar muscles. It causes ptosis and diplopia but does not present with optic nerve dysfunction, afferent pupillary defect, upper motor neuron signs, or MRI findings of white matter plaques. Treatment includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppression, not disease-modifying therapy for demyelination. Bottom line: Optic neuritis is a highly testable initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis, presenting with painful, acute, monocular vision loss and a relative afferent pupillary defect. MS should be considered in young women with CNS demyelination symptoms, including upper motor neuron findings and urinary incontinence. Pathology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

Front:
Subject System Topic A 5-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric neurology clinic for evaluation of gait abnormalities and poor coordination. Her parents report that she began walking later than other children and has always had trouble maintaining her balance. Over the past year, her teachers have noticed she struggles with tasks that require fine motor skills, such as using scissors or holding a pencil. The parents also note that she has
A. Excessive alcohol consumption B. History of spinal trauma C. Past diagnosis of renal carcinoma D. Prior episode of rheumatic fever E. Recent vegetarian dietary changes Syringomyelia - Cape-like lesion. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Chronic alcohol use is associated with several neurological complications, including cerebellar degeneration (leading to gait ataxia), peripheral neuropathy (with symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss), and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome due to thiamine deficiency. However, these conditions do not produce the classic dissociated sensory loss seen in this patient, where pain and temperature sensation are selectively impaired with preserved proprioception. Additionally, alcohol-related neuropathies typically begin distally in the lower limbs and progress proximally, rather than presenting with segmental upper limb weakness and sensory loss in a cape-like distribution. Choice C: Renal cell carcinoma is well known for paraneoplastic syndromes such as polycythemia due to erythropoietin secretion or hypercalcemia from PTHrP. It is also associated with metastases to bone, lung, and brain. However, it is not a recognized cause of syrinx formation or central cord pathology. Although renal cell carcinoma may rarely invade the spinal column or epidural space, such lesions typically produce cord compression, resulting in upper motor neuron signs like hyperreflexia and spasticity, rather than the flaccid weakness with selective sensory deficits seen in this patient. Choice D: Rheumatic fever is a post-streptococcal autoimmune condition that primarily affects the heart, joints, and central nervous system, with Sydenham chorea being its most notable neurologic manifestation. Sydenham chorea presents as involuntary, purposeless movements of the face and limbs, often in children and adolescents. It does not cause segmental motor atrophy, sensory dissociation, or spinal cord pathology. Rheumatic carditis may cause long-term valvular disease, but it is unrelated to the development of syringomyelia, which arises from structural or congenital abnormalities in CSF flow dynamics. Choice E: A vegetarian diet, if not well-balanced, may lead to vitamin B12 deficiency, which causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord. This condition typically affects the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts, leading to loss of vibration and proprioception, spastic weakness, and positive Babinski signs. It may also present with peripheral neuropathy. However, B12 deficiency does not spare proprioception while selectively impairing pain and temperature sensation. The pattern of cape-like dissociated sensory loss and lower motor neuron findings in the upper limbs seen here are not features of B12 deficiency. Bottom line: Syringomyelia is a central spinal cord disorder characterized by loss of pain and temperature sensation in a cape-like distribution with preserved proprioception, often accompanied by hand weakness and muscle atrophy. The most common acquired risk factor in adults is prior spinal trauma, which can lead to disruption of CSF flow and syrinx formation years later. Pathophysiology Neurology (Spinal cord disorders) None
Back:
D. Prior episode of rheumatic fever

The absence of telangiectasias, recurrent infections, and immune issues here argues against this diagnosis. Bottom line: Dandy–Walker malformation = congenital absence of cerebellar vermis + cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle. Presents in childhood with truncal ataxia, delayed motor milestones, and coordination difficulties. MRI confirms diagnosis; management is supportive unless hydrocephalus requires surgical intervention.

Choice B: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by a trinucleotide (CAG) repeat expansion in the HTT gene on chromosome 4. It presents with a triad of progressive choreiform movements, psychiatric disturbances (such as depression or irritability), and cognitive decline. Neuropathologically, there is degeneration of the caudate nucleus and putamen, with accompanying cortical atrophy. The disease typically manifests in mid-adulthood and worsens over 15–20 years. Huntington disease results from toxic gain-of-function of the huntingtin protein, not from iron deficiency or dopamine biosynthesis dysfunction. While both conditions may involve the basal ganglia, the movement features of Huntington disease are involuntary and hyperkinetic, unlike the voluntary leg movement behaviors seen in RLS. There is no increased risk of Huntington disease in patients with restless legs syndrome. Choice C: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system that primarily affects young adults, particularly women. It presents with neurologic deficits separated in time and space, such as optic neuritis, sensory loss, motor weakness, or cerebellar signs. MRI typically reveals periventricular white matter plaques, and CSF analysis may show oligoclonal bands. MS results from autoreactive T cells attacking myelin and is mediated by inflammatory cytokines. Although MS can involve various central pathways, including those responsible for movement and sensation, it does not impair dopamine synthesis or iron-dependent neurotransmission. RLS

Where on the following MRI is the location of this patient’s pathology?
A. A B. B C. C D. D Choice A = anterior horn of lateral ventricle; B = caudate nucleus; C = internal capsule; D = putamen; E = third ventricle; F = thalamus. E. E Wrong answer explanations: F. F Choice A: The anterior horn of the lateral ventricle contains cerebrospinal fluid and serves no direct role in motor regulation or cognitive function. Damage here would not result in Huntington disease but may reflect secondary ventricular enlargement due to adjacent caudate atrophy. The anterior horn is an anatomic landmark for neuroimaging but lacks inherent neurologic function. Common pathologies affecting this region include hydrocephalus and mass effect from frontal lobe tumors. Enlargement of the anterior horns, therefore, indicates volume loss or obstruction elsewhere rather than being a site of primary neurodegeneration. Choice C: The internal capsule transmits descending motor fibers from the cortex to the brainstem and spinal cord. Lesions here result in contralateral pure motor deficits due to corticospinal tract disruption rather than hyperkinetic movements. Lacunar infarcts from small vessel disease (e.g., hypertension, diabetes) are a key pathology affecting the internal capsule. Functionally, it is a critical white matter conduit with no role in regulating basal ganglia circuits, so it is uninvolved in Huntington disease pathophysiology. Typical presentations include hemiparesis or dysarthria-clumsy hand syndrome. Choice D: The putamen, along with the caudate, comprises the striatum and is affected in Huntington disease, though it tends to show atrophy later in the disease course. Early-stage Huntington disease primarily targets the caudate nucleus, making the putamen an incorrect answer in this case. The putamen is primarily involved in regulating voluntary movement via its connections to the globus pallidus. Classic pathology affecting the putamen includes Wilson disease (copper deposition) and chronic hypoxic injury, which may produce dystonia and parkinsonism rather than chorea. Choice E: The third ventricle lies in the midline between the thalami and plays no direct role in movement regulation or cognitive processing. Enlargement of the third ventricle typically reflects hydrocephalus or generalized brain atrophy rather than focal basal ganglia degeneration. It is frequently displaced in tumors of the pineal gland or colloid cysts at the foramen of Monro. While it may appear enlarged in Huntington disease due to adjacent atrophy, it is not the site of primary pathology. Choice F: The thalamus acts as a relay center for sensory and motor information and is not a primary site of pathology in Huntington disease. Thalamic strokes (e.g., paramedian artery infarcts) produce characteristic syndromes such as hemisensory loss and thalamic pain syndrome, distinct from chorea. Functionally, the thalamus modulates cortical input but does not degenerate early in Huntington disease. In later disease stages, thalamic atrophy may develop secondarily to widespread cortical and subcortical neuronal loss. Bottom line: Huntington disease is caused by degeneration of the caudate nucleus leading to chorea, psychiatric symptoms, and dementia, with MRI showing caudate atrophy and ventricular enlargement. The basal ganglia circuits maintain movement control, and their disruption produces characteristic hyperkinetic movement disorders when affected. The pathophysiology is believed to involve heightened sensitivity of peripheral nociceptors and myofascial tenderness rather than neurovascular activation. Compression of the geniculate ganglion by tumor Common precipitating factors include light touch to the face, chewing, talking, tooth brushing, shaving, and even exposure to wind. These innocuous stimuli activate hypersensitized mechanoreceptive afferents in the trigeminal nerve, triggering abnormal pain discharges. Between attacks, patients are typically asymptomatic, with normal cranial nerve function. Persistent sensory loss or weakness in the muscles of mastication should raise suspicion for secondary trigeminal neuralgia due to mass lesions, multiple sclerosis, or other structural pathology. C. Microvascular compression of the trigeminal nerve The clinical presentation typically involves one or more divisions of the trigeminal nerve—most commonly V2 (maxillary) and V3 (mandibular). The distribution is always unilateral, and bilateral symptoms are rare, except in the context of demyelinating disease such as multiple sclerosis. Diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by neuroimaging to D. Small-vessel ischemia in the brainstem sensory nuclei exclude secondary causes. First-line pharmacologic treatment is carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, both of which act by inhibiting voltage-gated sodium channels, thereby stabilizing neuronal membranes and reducing ectopic firing. Alternatives include lamotrigine and baclofen in patients who cannot tolerate first-line agents. In refractory cases, microvascular decompression is E. Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion the preferred surgical option, physically separating the offending vessel from the nerve root to relieve compression. Other procedures include percutaneous radiofrequency rhizotomy or gamma knife radiosurgery, which selectively damage pain fibers to reduce symptoms. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Axonal demyelination of facial motor fibers produces an acute, lower motor neuron palsy of cranial nerve VII with forehead involvement, loss of nasolabial fold, impaired eye closure, hyperacusis from stapedius weakness, and possible loss of taste on the anterior two thirds of the tongue. Pathology involves inflammatory demyelination of the facial nerve within the facial canal, leading to conduction block and flaccid facial weakness rather than paroxysmal pain. Symptoms evolve over hours to days and are not characteristically stimulus-triggered. Neurologic examination is abnormal between episodes in true facial nerve palsy. The vignette shows shock-like, touch-precipitated facial pain with a normal exam between attacks, which does not fit facial motor fiber demyelination. Choice B: Compression of the geniculate ganglion affects the sensory and parasympathetic components of the facial nerve, often causing deep otalgia, altered taste, decreased lacrimation, and progressive facial weakness from involvement of adjacent motor fibers. Tumor-related compression typically produces gradually worsening, persistent deficits rather than brief, electric, trigger-induced pain spells. Examination frequently reveals facial asymmetry or sensory changes, and imaging often shows a mass along the facial nerve pathway. The case features normal interictal neurologic findings and stimulus-evoked paroxysms in the trigeminal distribution, which are not explained by geniculate ganglion compression. Choice D: Small-vessel ischemia of brainstem sensory nuclei reflects lacunar pathology from lipohyalinosis or microatheroma in long-standing hypertension or diabetes, producing sustained sensory loss, dysesthesias, or ataxic syndromes depending on the tract involved. Deficits are continuous, often with accompanying objective signs on examination, and onset correlates with an ischemic event rather than dozens of brief attacks per day. Pain from central post-stroke phenomena is typically persistent or waxing–waning, not seconds-long shocks triggered by light touch. A normal neurologic examination between episodes and stimulus sensitivity argue strongly against a lacunar ischemic mechanism. Choice E: Viral reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion classically causes herpes zoster in the V1–V3 dermatomes, presenting with prodromal burning pain followed by a unilateral vesicular eruption on an erythematous base and possible ocular involvement with V1 disease. Pathogenesis involves latent varicella-zoster virus replicating within sensory neurons, leading to inflammation and persistent neuropathic pain that may evolve into postherpetic neuralgia. The clinical course features continuous or lingering pain rather than very brief, electric, triggerable paroxysms; skin findings are typical, and examination often reveals cutaneous lesions. Choice E: Traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage (tSAH) presents with blood in the sulci and basal cisterns on CT, often from cortical vessel tearing. It may cause headache, altered mental status, and sometimes seizure. However, it rarely leads to prolonged coma unless massive or associated with increased ICP. The CT here does not show the classic hyperdense signal in the basal cisterns or sulcal spaces expected in tSAH. Furthermore, this patient’s coma is disproportionate to the degree of hemorrhage, favoring axonal injury. Bottom line: Diffuse axonal injury occurs after severe deceleration trauma and leads to prolonged unconsciousness. CT may show punctate hemorrhages at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem; MRI is more sensitive. Diagnosis hinges on mechanism of injury, neurologic exam, and subtle radiologic findings—not overt hemorrhage or herniation.. Bottom line: During lumbar puncture between L3–L4, the ligamentum flavum must be traversed to access the spinal canal and is responsible for the characteristic "pop". Choice D: While stroke can cause confusion and altered mental status, it would typically present with focal neurologic deficits, such as unilateral weakness or speech disturbances, which are absent in this case. Stroke also does not present with trauma history, making it a less likely cause here. The crescent-shaped hemorrhage on CT is characteristic of subdural hematoma. Bottom line: Subdural hematoma results from rupture of bridging veins, often in elderly/dementia and alcoholics due to acceleration-deceleration trauma. CT imaging reveals a crescent-shaped hemorrhage, typically without a lucid interval, and symptoms may evolve slowly over time. Craniotomy is the definitive treatment. Choice C: The radial nerve innervates the extensor muscles of the forearm and wrist and is most commonly injured in midshaft humerus fractures. It presents with wrist drop, finger extension weakness, and sensory loss over the dorsum of the hand. This patient has none of these findings—his hand strength is intact and there is no mention of dorsal hand numbness. Furthermore, the primary motor deficit involves shoulder abduction, which is not controlled by the radial nerve. While label C accurately identifies the radial nerve, it is not implicated in this case. Choice D: The median nerve controls thumb opposition, wrist flexion, and forearm pronation, with sensory distribution in the lateral palm and digits 1–3. It is not responsible for shoulder movement and has no cutaneous branches to the upper arm. This patient has no difficulty with hand function or sensation in the median nerve territory. There are also no signs of carpal tunnel or pronator syndrome. Label D correctly marks the median nerve, but its function does not correspond to this presentation. Choice E: The ulnar nerve supplies most intrinsic hand muscles and provides sensation to the medial hand and digits 4–5. Injury results in grip weakness, claw hand deformities, and numbness of the pinky and half of the ring finger. These findings are absent in this patient, who has no hand weakness or sensory loss in the ulnar distribution. The motor deficit involves shoulder abduction, which the ulnar nerve does not control. Label E, identifying the ulnar nerve, is anatomically correct but irrelevant to the symptoms described. Bottom line:

Choice B: The deep peroneal nerve innervates muscles responsible for dorsiflexion (anterior compartment), such as tibialis anterior, and provides sensory innervation only to the first web space. An injury to this nerve would present with foot drop and loss of toe extension, neither of which are present in this case. Furthermore, this patient has normal dorsiflexion and no sensory loss between the first and second toes. Since eversion is lost but dorsiflexion is preserved, this eliminates deep peroneal nerve injury as a cause. Choice C: The saphenous nerve is a purely sensory branch of the femoral nerve and supplies the anteromedial aspect of the leg down to the medial foot. It does not innervate any muscles and does not provide sensation to the dorsum or lateral leg. It can be ruled out both by the motor deficit and by the sensory pattern in this case. Choice B: Median nerve injury is often due to supracondylar fractures of the humerus in children or carpal tunnel syndrome in adults. It supplies most of the forearm flexors and thenar muscles and provides sensation to the lateral 3½ fingers. Choice C: The musculocutaneous nerve arises from C5–C7 and innervates the anterior arm muscles like biceps brachii and brachialis. Injury results in weakened forearm flexion and loss of sensation over the lateral forearm. It is not typically injured in humeral fractures and does not contribute to wrist extension or hand sensation. Since wrist drop and dorsal hand paresthesia are not associated with this nerve, it is not the correct answer. Choice E: Ulnar nerve damage causes paresthesia in the medial 1½ fingers and weakness in interossei and hypothenar muscles. Compression at the wrist (Guyon canal) or elbow (cubital tunnel) are typical mechanisms. It does not innervate the wrist or finger extensors and thus does not cause wrist drop. The sensory loss in this patient is dorsal first web space, which is supplied by the superficial branch of the radial nerve—not the ulnar.

Choice E: Vertical gaze palsy is an inability to move the eyes up or down, often due to lesions affecting the dorsal midbrain. Parinaud syndrome, characterized by vertical gaze palsy, pupillary light-near dissociation, and eyelid retraction (Collier sign), is classically caused by a pinealoma compressing the superior colliculus.
